name: Special Series — Σn, Σn², Σn³ and AGP
whatIsIt: Some sums appear again and again and have neat formulas. Σn = 1+2+…+n = n(n+1)/2. Σn² = 1²+2²+…+n² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. Σn³ = 1³+2³+…+n³ = [n(n+1)/2]² — the SUM OF CUBES equals the SQUARE OF THE SUM. An ARITHMETIC–GEOMETRIC PROGRESSION (AGP) multiplies an AP term by a GP term, like 1, 2x, 3x², 4x³, …; its sum is found by the 'subtract r·S' trick.
whyNeeded: These standard sums shortcut long series questions in JEE and are the building blocks for summing many complicated series. Recognising them saves crucial exam time.
realLifeUses: Total dots in a triangular arrangement (Σn)
Total squares in a stacked pyramid (Σn²)
Adding up cube-shaped stock stacked in layers (Σn³)
Payments that grow both by a fixed amount and a fixed factor (AGP)

vocabulary.meaning: Sum of the first n natural numbers = n(n+1)/2.
Sum of the first n squares = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6.
Sum of the first n cubes = [n(n+1)/2]² = (Σn)².
Arithmetic–geometric progression: an AP term times a GP term, e.g. 1, 2x, 3x², …

story.title: Stacking Cubes
story.text: Naina stacks cube blocks: 1 in the top layer, then 8, then 27. 'Adding cubes is slow,' she sighs. Bhaiya smiles: 'Use the formula! Σn³ equals the SQUARE of Σn. For 3 layers, Σn = 6, so Σn³ = 6² = 36 — no adding.' Naina checks 1 + 8 + 27 = 36. 'Whoa,' she grins, 'the sum of cubes is just the sum, squared.'
story.extractedProblem: Naina stacks 1 + 8 + 27 cube blocks. Using Σn³ = (Σn)², what is the total?

visual.caption: For n = 3: Σn = 6, Σn² = 14, Σn³ = 36. Notice Σn³ (36) is exactly (Σn)² = 6² — the sum of cubes is the square of the sum.

standardMethod.summary: Match the series to a standard sum and apply its formula: Σn = n(n+1)/2, Σn² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6, Σn³ = [n(n+1)/2]². For an AGP, multiply the whole sum by the common ratio r and subtract to collapse it.
steps: Identify whether you're adding n, n², or n³ (or a mix).
Apply the matching formula for that sum.
For a mixed series, split it: Σ(an² + bn + c) = aΣn² + bΣn + cn.
For an AGP sum S, compute S − rS to telescope, then solve for S.

workedExamples.problem: Find Σn³ for n = 3 using Σn³ = (Σn)².

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the Σn formula n(n+1)/2 when adding squares.
commonMistakes.fix: Σn² has its OWN formula: n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 — not n(n+1)/2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting that the sum of cubes equals the square of the sum.
commonMistakes.fix: Σn³ = [n(n+1)/2]² = (Σn)². Compute Σn first, then square it.

teachBackPrompt: Stack cube blocks in layers (1, then 8, then 27) and add them: 36. Then show a grown-up that Σn = 1+2+3 = 6 and 6² = 36 too — explaining why the sum of cubes equals the square of the sum.

revisionCard.summary: Standard sums: Σn = n(n+1)/2; Σn² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6; Σn³ = [n(n+1)/2]² = (Σn)². Split mixed series into these pieces. For an AGP sum S, use S − rS to telescope.

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 11 / JEE Main & Advanced (special series & AGP)

rememberIt.unpack: Cubes sum to the square of the sum. Squares have the /6 formula, not /2.

studentQuestions.q: How do I sum a series like Σ(2n² + 3n)?
studentQuestions.a: Split it: 2·Σn² + 3·Σn, then plug in the standard formulas — much faster than adding term by term.

studentQuestions.q: What makes an AGP different from an AP or GP?
studentQuestions.a: Each term is an AP term TIMES a GP term (like n·xⁿ⁻¹), so it grows both additively and multiplicatively; you sum it with the S − rS trick.
